questions.question: Explain the hierarchical directory structure and give an example of an operating system that uses it.
keyPoints: Hierarchical directory: tree-like organization with a root directory and subdirectories.
Example OS: UNIX, Linux (e.g., ext4), Windows (e.g., NTFS).
Advantages: easy organization, simple navigation, and clear file naming.

questions.question: What is the difference between contiguous and linked file allocation? Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each.
keyPoints: Contiguous allocation: files occupy sequential blocks, allowing fast random access but suffers from external fragmentation.
Linked allocation: blocks are linked via pointers, eliminating external fragmentation but providing poor random access.
Advantage of contiguous: fast random access; disadvantage: external fragmentation.
Advantage of linked: no external fragmentation; disadvantage: slow random access.

questions.question: Discuss the three main types of directory structures: hierarchical, acyclic graph, and general graph. Explain the advantages and disadvantages of each and give practical examples of OS file systems that use each type. Also, describe the methods for directory implementation.
keyPoints: Hierarchical: tree structure with root and subdirectories, simple navigation, no sharing; used in UNIX, Linux (ext4), Windows (NTFS).
Acyclic graph: allows shared subdirectories/hard links, no cycles, enables file sharing; example UNIX (hard links), Windows (shortcuts).
General graph: permits cycles, requires cycle detection/garbage collection, rarely used; example older OS like Multics.
Directory implementation: linear list (simple, linear search) vs hash table (fast lookup, collision handling); trade-offs between speed and memory.

questions.question: Describe the various file allocation methods (contiguous, linked, indexed) and free space management techniques (bit vector, linked list, grouping, counting). Explain how each method works and compare their performance in terms of external fragmentation, access time, and ease of implementation.
keyPoints: Contiguous allocation: sequential blocks, easy random access, external fragmentation.
Linked allocation: blocks linked via pointers, no external fragmentation, poor random access.
Indexed allocation: uses index block for each file, supports direct access, overhead of index block.
Free space management: bit vector (fast but memory-intensive), linked list (simple but slow), grouping (efficient), counting (compact).
